text:
CT NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — 2-YEAR POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE

CLINICAL INDICATION:
54-year-old woman with history of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0 per AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive staging), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation (IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions with concurrent cisplatin, completed approximately 24 months prior). Prior imaging demonstrated complete metabolic response on 12-week post-treatment PET-CT, with subsequent 6-month and 12-month surveillance imaging negative for recurrence. Patient presents for 2-year surveillance CT neck. Comparison made to prior CT neck performed at 6-month post-treatment surveillance and 12-month post-treatment PET-CT.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the neck was performed without and with intravenous contrast (iohexol, 100 mL, administered via peripheral IV at 3 mL/sec). Multiplanar reformatted images were obtained in the axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Slice thickness: 2.5 mm. No acute allergic reaction to contrast. Oral contrast was not administered.

FINDINGS:

Primary Site — Base of Tongue and Oropharynx:
The left base of tongue demonstrates stable post-radiation mucosal thickening and mild soft tissue fullness when compared to the 6-month post-treatment study. There is no discrete mass, asymmetric nodularity, or abnormal enhancement to suggest recurrent or residual neoplasm at the primary site. The mucosa of the base of tongue, valleculae, and the remainder of the visible oropharynx appears symmetrical without a focal enhancing lesion. No erosion of the underlying musculature or involvement of the pre-epiglottic space is identified. The epiglottis and supraglottic structures are unremarkable.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged cervical lymph nodes are identified bilaterally. The previously documented left level IIA lymph node, which measured 2.1 cm at diagnosis and had progressively decreased to 0.7 cm on the 6-month post-treatment study, is again seen as a small, fatty-hila-bearing node now measuring approximately 0.6 cm in short axis — well within normal limits and stable to slightly smaller compared to prior examination. No new abnormal lymph nodes are seen in the bilateral levels I through V, the retropharyngeal, or the bilateral supraclavicular regions. No evidence of extracapsular extension.

Post-Radiation Tissue Changes:
Cervical soft tissue fibrosis is again noted along the bilateral anterior and lateral neck, consistent with known post-radiation changes and stable in extent compared to prior studies. There is mild diffuse thickening of the platysma and subcutaneous tissues of the bilateral neck. No new areas of abnormal enhancement or fluid collection are identified within the post-radiation soft tissue bed.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland appears mildly heterogeneous and slightly reduced in volume compared to prior examination, compatible with post-radiation thyroid change in the setting of known radiation-related hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. No discrete thyroid nodules above the threshold for characterization are identified. No suspicious focal thyroid lesions.

Salivary Glands:
The bilateral parotid and submandibular glands appear mildly atrophic and fatty replaced bilaterally, in keeping with post-radiation sialadenopathy. This is stable compared to prior imaging. No focal masses or ductal obstruction.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral carotid arteries are patent. Mild wall thickening of the bilateral common carotid arteries is noted, likely related to prior radiation exposure. No critical luminal stenosis or thrombosis identified on this non-dedicated vascular study.

Airway:
The upper airway remains patent at the level of the nasopharynx, oropharynx, hypopharynx, and supraglottis. No subglottic narrowing. No tracheal deviation.

Osseous Structures:
The imaged cervical spine demonstrates mild multilevel degenerative changes at C4-C5 and C5-C6 without high-grade neural foraminal compromise. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion. No pathological fracture.

IMPRESSION:

1. No imaging evidence of recurrent or residual primary oropharyngeal malignancy. The left base of tongue demonstrates only stable post-treatment changes without a suspicious enhancing mass.

2. No pathologically enlarged cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. The previously treated left level IIA node remains small and within normal limits, unchanged from the prior study.

3. Stable post-radiation soft tissue fibrosis and fatty changes of the bilateral salivary glands, consistent with the known treatment history and unchanged from prior surveillance imaging.

4. Mild thyroid volume reduction consistent with post-radiation hypothyroidism, stable.

5. Mild bilateral carotid wall thickening, a known late sequela of neck irradiation; clinical correlation and routine cardiovascular risk factor management as appropriate per the treating team.

Overall, the 2-year post-treatment neck CT is reassuring without evidence of locoregional recurrence or new nodal disease.
